Farn Polrrrcer- PRACTrces CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl1_232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 4,2009
Shirlee Zane
Third District Supervisor
County of Sonoma Board <if Supervisors
575 Administration Drive, RM. l00A
Santa Rosa, CA 95403
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-180
Dear Ms. Zate:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Politicil Practices Commission (..the
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of oiher conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Government code Section 1090.
QUESTTON
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from making,
participating in, or using your position to influence a governmental decisionlhut *uy
have a financial effect on your adult son or the company for which he works?
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections g lff)O through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationf of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in sections l8 I l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the california
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-09-180
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Generally, a public official does not have a conflict of interest under the Act
solely because the official makes, participates in, or influences a governmental decision
that has a financial effect on his or her adult child or a company for which the adult child
works. For the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions to apply, the official must have a
specific economic interest in the child or in the company for which the child works.2
T.ACTS
You are a member of the Board of Supervisors for the County of Sonoma (the
"Board of Supervisors"). The Board of Supervisors will soon be considering a proposed
fee on aggregate mining operators within the County. One operator, who miy be suUlect
to the fee, is Syar lndustries, [nc. ("Syar"). Syar is one of the larger mining operations in
the County.
Your adult so.n, who is [8 years of age or older, is employed part time with Syar's
vineyard operations.' Your adult son currently lives at your home and is a student. you
also provide his housing and pay for his education, food, and transportation costs.
Although you did not claim your adult son as your dependent on your 2008 income tax
return, you will be declaring him as a dependent on your 2009 return.
While you do not currently know whether Syar's vineyard operations are a
separate corporate entity form Syar, it is your understanding that the same key personal
own and/or operate both the aggregate operations and the vineyard operations. Other
than your adult son's employment, neither you nor your family has any other interests in
Syar.
AI\ALYSIS
Section 8710O prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental deiision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest,'in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the iublic official's
economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The commission has
2 We emphasize that this conclusion is limited to the provisions of the Act. your participation in
the decisions you have described may be prohibited by other provisions of law including cormmon law
conflict-of-interest laws and Government Code Section 1090. We suggest you consult *ith you, county,s
counsel on these issues.
.3 Speaking on your behalf, your statTassistant, Jessica Diaz, stated that your son is lg year ofage
or older in a telephone conversation on July 29,2009.


File No. A-09-180
Page No. 3
adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in a given gorc*-ental decision.
Step One: Are you a.,public official?r'
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bX1).)- A:'pubiic official" is ..every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . ..',
(Section 82048.) As a member of the Board of Supervisors, you are a public official
within the meaning of the Act.a
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrlactual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the goveninental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to irr. hi-s or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 187 02.3.) As a member of the Board of supervisors, you *. rriukirg,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision if you take part in a
decision relating to your son's company Syar.
Step Three: What are your economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest,, in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the oficial's economic
interests, described as follows:
, 
a If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*87zo}filers" include members of a
county board of supervisors) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a p;blic
meeting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each
type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic inteiest, as discussed
in Regulation 18702.5(bX l)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave
the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars.
absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in
Regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) appty. -(Section g7105.)


File No. A-09-180
Page No.4
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a); Regulation lg703.l(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,-or holds'any
position of managemenr. (section g7103(d); Regulation tgzoi.tG).)s
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(b); Regulation tg703.2.)
o An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to tie decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3.)
o An economic interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section SZf 6Ste);
Regulation t87O3.4.)
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103; Regulation I g703.5.)
An official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision if the
decision has a financial effect on a membeiof the official's "immediate family.;, Section
82029 defines "immediate family" as the official's "spouse and dependent children.,,
However, whether a child is claimed as a dependent on an income tax return does not in
itself determine whether a child is a dependent under Section gzo2g. For purposes of
Section 82029, we have previously advised that "dependent children" are ihose children
who are under l8 and whom the official can claim as a dependent for federal income tax
purposes. (See e.g., Tremlett Advice Letter, No. t-g9-3g6.) Accordingly, you do not
have a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision that has a financial-efiect on your
adult son, who is over 18 years of age, oron the company for which he works solely
because he is your son. Instead, you would only havi a iotential conflict of interest in
the decision if the decision affected one of the five economic interests iOentifiea aUore.6
s We note that an official with an economic interest in a business entity including a source of
income that is a business entity also has economic interests in any parent, subsidiary, or ot'herwise related
business entity. (Regulations 18703.1 and 18703.3.) Even ifSyar;s vineyard op"rutio6 are operated as a
sep?rate corporate entity, your account of the facts indicates that the Syar-'s vineyard operation's are related
to Syar under Regulation 18703.1(dX2) because you state that both thi aggregate and the vineyard
operations are owned/operated by the same key personal. Accordingly. iny official with an economic
interest in Syar's vineyard operations would aiso have an economicTri"..ri i, syur.
u N.ote that you are subject to disqualification from a decision if you have an economic interest in
your son such as an economic interest in your son as a source of income. (Section g7103(c); Regulation
18703'3') You are not, however,,subject to disqualification from a decision based upon on .lonorl.
interest in your son as a source ofgifts because ihe te.m "gift," as defined by the Act, does not include
payments from "an individual's spouse, child, parent, grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parenrin-


File No. A-09-180
Page No. 5
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
law, brother-in-law, sister-in-law, nephew, niece, aunt, uncle, or first cousin or the spouse of any such
person. . .." (Section 82028(b)(3).)

QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from making,
participating in, or using your position to influence a governmental decisionlhut *uy
have a financial effect on your adult son or the company for which he works?

CONCLUSION: Generally, a public official does not have a conflict of interest under the Act
solely because the official makes, participates in, or influences a governmental decision
that has a financial effect on his or her adult child or a company for which the adult child
works. For the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions to apply, the official must have a
specific economic interest in the child or in the company for which the child works.2

to the fee, is Syar lndustries, [nc. ("Syar"). Syar is one of the larger mining operations in
the County.
Your adult so.n, who is [8 years of age or older, is employed part time with Syar's
vineyard operations.' Your adult son currently lives at your home and is a student. you
also provide his housing and pay for his education, food, and transportation costs.
Although you did not claim your adult son as your dependent on your 2008 income tax
return, you will be declaring him as a dependent on your 2009 return.
While you do not currently know whether Syar's vineyard operations are a
separate corporate entity form Syar, it is your understanding that the same key personal
own and/or operate both the aggregate operations and the vineyard operations. Other
than your adult son's employment, neither you nor your family has any other interests in
Syar.
